name: Clara Siagian
affiliation: Bartlett Development Planning Unit, University College London
tagline:
Hi, I'm Clara Siagian. I’m an early career scholar whose work asks how young people experience, navigate, and contest the multifaceted forces that shape their lives. 

I am currently Postdoctoral Researcher at the Bartlett Development Planning Unit, University College London, and a Research Affiliate with Universitas Indonesia at the Center on Child Protection and Wellbeing (PUSKAPA)
bio:
My work sits at the intersection of urban politics, youth studies, public policy, and feminist scholarship, with a sustained empirical focus on Indonesia and the wider Asia Pacific region. Across my works, I ask, how policies become lived social realities through everyday negotiations, and how might we make these negotiations knowable? Whose experiential knowledge counts in the dominant governance paradigm? I pursue these questions through participatory and ethnographic methods that treat informants, particularly young people, as knowledge producers whose interpretations are central to how we understand policy and power. 

I completed my PhD at the Crawford School of Public Policy, Australian National University, in November 2024. My doctoral research examined the lived consequences of forced eviction and relocation to state-managed social housing in Jakarta, tracing how displaced residents understand the state and make political claims in the wake of their resettlement. It has been published in Q1 journals such as Cities and IJURR.

As senior researcher, and now research affiliate, with the Centre on Child Protection and Wellbeing (PUSKAPA), Universitas Indonesia, my works explore the intersection of young people, social polices, development interventions in partnership with DFAT-Australia, WHO, IDRC Canada, UNFPA, and UNICEF.

My research has been recognised through competitive awards, including the Australian Government Research Training Program (AGRTP, 2019–2023), the Asian Studies Association of Australia Postdoctoral Writing Grant, and selection as a SEAREG New Faces Fellow at Duke University. I have published in a number of Q1 journal such as Cities and Children’s Geographies.

Beyond academic works, I also served as consultant on GEDSI and social impact assessment for KONEKSI-DFAT Australia and the World Bank.
methods: Long-term ethnographic fieldwork; longitudinal qualitative panels; oral history; participatory mapping with young people; and policy-engaged research with civil society and government partners.
